caption:
TypeScript's 'satisfies' keyword changed how I write types.

It validates your type WITHOUT widening it.
'as' just trusts you blindly.

One catches bugs at compile time. The other hides them.

📌 SAVE this — it'll fix your next TypeScript headache.

Did you know about satisfies? Comment 👇

#typescript #javascript #webdev #coding #programming #types #frontend #react #nextjs #codingtips

slides.title: Which snippet is better?
slides.problem: In TypeScript, define a config object with strict types.

a.label: as (type assertion)

b.label: satisfies (type validation)

verdicts.title: Silently lies to the compiler
verdicts.text: 'as' tells TypeScript: trust me, I know what I'm doing. It won't check if debug: 'yes' matches boolean. The bug ships to production.

verdicts.title: Validates AND preserves the narrow type
verdicts.text: 'satisfies' checks that your object matches Config — and throws a compile error for debug: 'yes'. But unlike 'as', it keeps the literal types. config.port is 3000, not just number.

slides.title: The real difference
slides.sub: satisfies validates without widening your types:
slides.note: satisfies = validation + narrow types. Best of both worlds.

items.text: as → trusts you blindly, hides type errors
satisfies → validates AND keeps literal types
as const → makes everything readonly + literal
satisfies + as const → strictest possible typing

slides.teaser: Next: 3 TypeScript patterns seniors use daily
